source_inspiration.name: Dicklesworthstone/pi_agent_rust
source_inspiration.notes: Rust CLI port of Pi agent; single-binary focus; 7 built-in tools; JSONL session tree; RPC mode; capability-gated extension runtime.

source_inspiration.name: badlogic/pi-mono
source_inspiration.notes: Original TypeScript monorepo: coding-agent CLI + unified LLM API + UI libraries + Slack bot + vLLM pods; extensible via TS Extensions/Skills/Prompt Templates/Themes.

source_inspiration.name: dmtrKovalenko/fff.nvim
source_inspiration.notes: High-performance Rust-backed fuzzy file picker with persistent index, frecency, git status, and typo-resistant search.

context: pi-mono offers a flexible toolkit but is built on a JS/TS runtime stack.
pi_agent_rust demonstrates a fast Rust CLI but search/indexing and toolkit breadth can be improved and unified.
Large monorepos need faster file/code search and completion than periodic directory walks and small caps.
pain_points: High memory/latency and cold-start costs in JS-heavy agent toolchains.
Inconsistent behavior and complexity across multiple repos/languages.
Slow or limited file completion/search in large codebases.
Hard-to-audit extension/tool permissions; need capability-based isolation.
opportunity: Deliver a cohesive, native Rust agent toolkit with strong performance and memory safety.
Provide a first-class search subsystem (file picker + grep) with persistent indexing, frecency, git integration, and typo-tolerant fuzzy matching.
Preserve Pi’s extensibility model (extensions/skills/prompts/themes) while making the core runtime Rust-native.

vision: A minimal-but-powerful coding agent harness that is natively Rust: fast startup, low memory, predictable streaming, safe-by-default tools, and an extensible plugin ecosystem—without requiring Node/Bun at runtime.

goals.title: Native Rust core + single-binary CLI
goals.detail: Ship a single `pi` executable with an embeddable Rust library (`pi-core`) and stable streaming UX.

goals.title: Unified LLM API in Rust
goals.detail: Provide a provider-agnostic interface with model discovery, tool-calling, token/cost tracking, streaming, abort/resume, and OpenAI-compatible endpoints.

goals.title: High-performance file + code search
goals.detail: Implement persistent indexing with typo-resistant fuzzy path search, frecency and git-aware ranking, and fast content search with plain/regex/fuzzy modes.

goals.title: Extensibility without forking
goals.detail: Support skills/prompts/themes/packages and extensions with capability-based isolation. Default: WASM components; optional: JS/TS compatibility runtime for existing ecosystem.

goals.title: Multiple execution surfaces
goals.detail: Interactive TUI, print mode, and a headless JSON-RPC/stdio mode for IDEs and custom frontends.

goals.title: Toolchain-quality testing
goals.detail: Conformance suite for extensions, deterministic RPC protocol tests, search benchmarks, and robust integration tests for tool sandboxing.

non_goals: Build a full IDE or editor (Pi remains a harness; integrations happen via RPC/SDK).
Implement autonomous 'sub-agent swarms' as a default behavior (kept as optional via extensions).
Ship proprietary provider credentials handling beyond standard OAuth/API-key flows.
Replace ripgrep entirely; instead, provide an optimized default path with optional delegation.

user_stories.story: As a terminal developer, I can start an interactive `pi` session that streams responses and tool activity smoothly.
acceptance_criteria: Streaming begins without blocking the UI.
Tool calls are rendered with clear boundaries and errors are readable.
Abort stops current work and returns to prompt within 250ms after tool boundary.

user_stories.story: As a terminal developer, I can run `pi -p "..."` to get a single answer suitable for scripts (no TUI).
acceptance_criteria: Writes answer to stdout; errors to stderr.
Non-interactive exit codes are stable and documented.

user_stories.story: As a terminal developer, I can reference files using `@path` with fast, typo-tolerant completion.
acceptance_criteria: Completion works in repos with 50k+ files.
Ranking prefers recently opened/modified files and relevant git statuses.
Completion updates incrementally on filesystem changes.

user_stories.story: As a terminal developer, my sessions persist on disk and support branching and navigation.
acceptance_criteria: Sessions stored as an append-only log with stable schema and versioning.
I can continue most recent session and open a specific session by path.
I can branch from a prior turn without losing history.

user_stories.story: As an integrator, I can control the agent via a line-delimited JSON protocol (stdin/stdout) and receive streaming events.
acceptance_criteria: Protocol supports prompt/steer/follow-up/abort/get-state/compact.
Protocol is versioned with backward-compatible evolution rules.
Malformed input yields structured error events without crashing.

user_stories.story: As a developer, I can switch providers/models and keep token/cost accounting per session.
acceptance_criteria: Model registry lists available tool-capable models per provider.
Costs/tokens recorded in session metadata and exported as JSON.

user_stories.story: As a security admin, I can apply a policy preset (safe/balanced/permissive) that governs tool and extension capabilities.
acceptance_criteria: Policies can be inspected and explained ('why was this denied?').
All denied actions are logged with context.
Default posture is safe-by-default.

user_stories.story: As an extension author, I can add a custom tool and command without forking core.
acceptance_criteria: Extensions can register tools, commands, and event hooks.
Extensions can be hot-reloaded in interactive mode.
Extensions run with no ambient OS access; hostcalls are capability-gated.

user_stories.story: As a developer, I can install and use skills that follow the Agent Skills standard and are loaded on-demand.
acceptance_criteria: Skills are discovered from standard locations and can be disabled.
Skill frontmatter is validated with warnings; missing description blocks loading.
Skills are progressively disclosed (only description always in context).

user_stories.story: As a developer, I can search code content interactively with plain/regex/fuzzy modes and get highlights.
acceptance_criteria: Plain mode is fast and does not compile regex.
Regex mode reports a readable error and can fall back safely.
Fuzzy mode ranks by match score and returns highlight spans.

user_stories.story: As a team, we can run a Slack bot that routes messages into pi sessions and posts results with context.
acceptance_criteria: Bot supports per-channel/per-thread sessions.
Sensitive capabilities can be disabled.

content_search: time budgets to avoid UI freezes

capabilities: fs access (no direct, only via tool hostcalls)

quality.rpc_compatibility: no breaking changes within a major version

risks.risk: Maintaining compatibility with existing TS extension ecosystem may complicate the Rust-native runtime.
risks.mitigation: Treat JS/TS runtime as optional compatibility layer; make WASM the primary long-term path; provide automated conformance tests.

risks.risk: Persistent indexing can introduce corruption or edge-case correctness issues.
risks.mitigation: Use checksummed records, health-check on startup, and automatic rebuild; keep index append-only where possible.

risks.risk: Provider APIs evolve quickly (models, endpoints, auth).
risks.mitigation: Plugin-like provider modules; strong contract tests with recorded fixtures; OpenAI-compatible adapter as escape hatch.

open_questions: Should the primary async runtime be tokio, asupersync, or an abstraction over both?
How much TS extension compatibility is required on day 1 vs later?
Which session format version is adopted: strict JSONL-only vs JSONL + sidecar DB by default?
Should content search delegate to ripgrep by default on very large repos, or always use internal grep engine?
